questions.stem: In a backend interview, what does a reverse proxy typically provide in front of app servers?
options: TLS termination, load balancing, routing, and buffering for upstream services
Compiling application source code on every HTTP request
Storing only client cookies inside the CPU L1 cache
Replacing relational databases with spreadsheet files
questions.explanation: Reverse proxies sit at the edge and forward traffic to application instances.

questions.stem: Stateless application servers behind a load balancer — why prefer them?
options: Sessions must live only in process memory forever
Any instance can handle any request, simplifying horizontal scale and failover
Sticky sessions become mandatory for every endpoint
You cannot use JWT or external session stores at all
questions.explanation: Statelessness lets the balancer route freely without affinity constraints.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: When explaining vertical vs horizontal scaling, what's accurate? Which answer is best?
options: Horizontal always means increasing CPU clock on one box
Vertical means adding more microservices only
Vertical: bigger machine; horizontal: more machines sharing the load
They are identical terms in cloud architecture docs
questions.explanation: Scale up vs scale out is the classic vertical/horizontal distinction.

questions.stem: Connection pooling for databases — interview rationale?
options: Each query must open and close a fresh TCP connection always
Pools eliminate the need for indexes on hot tables
Pooling replaces transactions with eventual consistency
Reusing open connections avoids expensive handshake/setup per query
questions.explanation: Pools amortize connection cost and bound concurrent DB load.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Idempotent PUT/DELETE in APIs — why does backend care? Which answer is best?
options: Safe retries after timeouts won't double-apply side effects
Idempotency means responses can never include bodies
Only GET may ever be retried by clients or proxies
Databases forbid unique constraints when APIs are idempotent
questions.explanation: Retries are common; idempotent writes prevent duplicate mutations.

questions.stem: ACID — which expansion is correct for interviews?
options: Availability, Consistency, Integrity, Distribution for CAP only
Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability for transaction guarantees
Async, Cached, Indexed, Durable as storage engine modes
Append, Compact, Isolate, Delete as log policies
questions.explanation: ACID describes relational transaction properties clients rely on.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: BASE in eventual-consistency systems stands for? Which answer is best?
options: Binary Atomic Strong Execution as a SQL mode
Buffered Append Streaming Engine for Kafka only
Basically Available, Soft state, Eventual consistency
Blocking ACID Serial Execution for all NoSQL
questions.explanation: BASE describes availability-first designs that converge later.

questions.stem: In an interview setting, consider this question: CAP theorem interview-safe statement?? Pick the strongest answer.
options: You can always have perfect C, A, and P simultaneously in practice
CAP only applies to single-node embedded databases
Partition tolerance is optional on the public internet
In a network partition, a distributed system must trade off consistency vs availability
questions.explanation: Partitions happen; choose CP or AP behavior for that window.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Optimistic locking typical mechanism? Which answer is best?
options: Version/etag columns; update fails if the version changed concurrently
SELECT ... FOR UPDATE on every read without exception
Disabling concurrent writers at the load balancer forever
Storing locks only in the browser localStorage
questions.explanation: Optimistic concurrency detects conflicts via versions instead of holding locks long.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Pessimistic locking when? Which answer is best?
options: Read-only dashboards with no writers ever
High contention updates where conflicts are likely and costly to retry
Static asset CDN caching exclusively
Generating OpenAPI docs from code comments
questions.explanation: Pessimistic locks serialize access when conflict rates make optimism wasteful.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Message queue role in backend architecture? Which answer is best?
options: Replace TLS for all service-to-service traffic
Guarantee exactly-once delivery in every broker by default
Decouple producers/consumers, buffer spikes, and enable async work
Eliminate the need for databases entirely
questions.explanation: Queues smooth load and separate write path from slow downstream work.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: At-least-once delivery implies what consumer duty? Which answer is best?
options: Assume each message arrives exactly one time always
Ack before processing to maximize throughput safely
Disable retries so duplicates never appear
Make handlers idempotent because duplicates can be delivered
questions.explanation: At-least-once + retries ⇒ design for duplicate-safe processing.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Cache-aside (lazy loading) pattern steps? Which answer is best?
options: App reads cache; on miss, load DB, populate cache, return value
DB always writes through cache before acknowledging clients
Cache invalidates the entire cluster on every SELECT
Browsers own the only cache; servers never cache
questions.explanation: Cache-aside keeps the app responsible for read-through population.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Write-through cache — key property? Which answer is best?
options: Writes only hit memory and never reach durable storage
Writes update cache and backing store together before success returns
Reads always bypass cache to avoid stale data
TTL must be infinite for write-through to work
questions.explanation: Write-through keeps cache and store in sync on each write path.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Thundering herd / cache stampede mitigation? Which answer is best?
options: Delete all keys whenever any one expires
Disable caching for popular keys only
Singleflight/locks, probabilistic early refresh, or staggered TTLs
Set every TTL to one millisecond globally
questions.explanation: Prevent many clients from stampeding the DB on the same miss.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: JWT for API auth — what should you mention carefully? Which answer is best?
options: JWTs are always stored only on the server like classic sessions
Signatures make payload encryption unnecessary for secrets inside
Expired tokens must still be accepted for offline apps
Client holds signed claims; validate signature/exp; revocation needs extra design
questions.explanation: JWTs are bearer tokens — validate crypto and plan logout/revocation.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Session cookies vs JWT — fair trade-off line? Which answer is best?
options: Server sessions are easy to revoke; JWTs scale statelessly but revoke is harder
Cookies cannot be HttpOnly or Secure in modern browsers
JWTs eliminate all need for HTTPS on public networks
Server sessions never require a shared store in multi-instance apps
questions.explanation: Revocation and storage location drive the session vs token choice.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: OAuth 2.0 authorization code flow — primary purpose? Which answer is best?
options: Replace TLS certificates with bearer tokens in the clear
Let a client obtain access tokens for a user without handling their password
Encrypt database rows using the authorization code as the key
Authenticate SSH sessions using only refresh tokens
questions.explanation: Auth code flow delegates user auth to the IdP and issues tokens to apps.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Rate limiting at the API gateway — why? Which answer is best?
options: Increase latency artificially to improve perceived performance
Replace authentication for all public endpoints
Protect backends from abuse, noisy neighbors, and accidental overload
Guarantee fairness without any token bucket or counter
questions.explanation: Limits defend availability and cost when traffic is uneven or hostile.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Structured logging best practice for services? Which answer is best?
options: Print only free-form prose without timestamps or levels
Log plaintext passwords for easier support debugging
Disable logs in production to save disk always
Emit JSON/key-value logs with request IDs for correlation across hops
questions.explanation: Structured, correlated logs make distributed debugging tractable.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: N+1 query problem — how do you describe and fix it? Which answer is best?
options: Looping loads cause one query per child; fix with joins/eager load/batch fetch
Using one query for an entire report is always an N+1 bug
Connection pools cause N+1 by multiplexing sockets
Indexes create N+1 because the planner visits leaves
questions.explanation: N+1 is chatty ORM access; batch or join to cut round trips.

questions.stem: Database index trade-off in one interview sentence?
options: Indexes only slow SELECT and never affect INSERT
Speed reads/lookups at the cost of slower writes and extra storage
Every column should be indexed without measuring workload
Hash indexes always beat B-trees for range scans
questions.explanation: Indexes are a write/space tax paid for faster targeted reads.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Read replicas — typical use and caveat? Which answer is best?
options: Replicas always provide stronger consistency than the primary
Writes should prefer replicas to reduce primary load
Scale read traffic; replicas may lag so stale reads are possible
Replication removes the need for backups entirely
questions.explanation: Async replicas help reads but introduce replication lag.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Sharding a database — core idea? Which answer is best?
options: Copy the full dataset to every node for every query
Store only indexes on shards and rows on one primary forever
Sharding eliminates the need to choose a primary key
Partition data across nodes by a shard key to scale storage and write load
questions.explanation: Shards split the dataset; key design dominates operational pain.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Saga pattern for distributed transactions? Which answer is best?
options: Sequence local transactions with compensating actions on failure
Use a single global XA lock across all microservices always
Avoid compensations by ignoring partial failures
Saga requires a shared memory bus between all JVMs
questions.explanation: Sagas coordinate multi-service workflows without distributed 2PC.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Two-phase commit downside you'd cite? Which answer is best?
options: It never provides atomicity across multiple resources
Blocking, coordinator failures, and poor fit for high-latency services
It only works for eventually consistent document stores
Participants never need to vote or prepare
questions.explanation: 2PC is correct but operationally heavy and fragile under partitions.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Health checks: liveness vs readiness? Which answer is best?
options: They are identical probes with different names only
Readiness kills the process; liveness only removes from LB
Liveness: restart if dead; readiness: stop routing until dependencies are OK
Neither should ever check dependency connectivity
questions.explanation: Orchestrators use both probes for different failure modes.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Graceful shutdown for HTTP services — what do you do? Which answer is best?
options: Kill -9 immediately on SIGTERM for fastest deploys
Ignore signals and let the LB time out clients
Drop keep-alives but finish accepting unlimited new connections
Stop taking new work, drain in-flight requests, then exit cleanly
questions.explanation: Drain connections so deploys don't sever active requests.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Circuit breaker vs retry — complementary roles? Which answer is best?
options: Retries handle transient blips; breakers stop calling a sick dependency
Breakers replace all retries and timeouts permanently
Retries should ignore backoff and hammer failing hosts
Circuit breakers only apply to disk I/O inside one process
questions.explanation: Use retries carefully and trip breakers when failure rates spike.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Bulkhead pattern in backends? Which answer is best?
options: Share one connection pool across every outbound client forever
Isolate resources (pools/threads) so one dependency failure can't starve all
Put all traffic on a single thread for easier reasoning
Disable timeouts so slow calls never free workers
questions.explanation: Bulkheads contain blast radius via partitioned resources.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: API gateway responsibilities commonly include? Which answer is best?
options: Owning all business rules and database schemas exclusively
Replacing service meshes for east-west mTLS always
Authn/z, routing, rate limits, TLS, and request aggregation at the edge
Compiling frontend bundles on each API call
questions.explanation: Gateways centralize cross-cutting edge concerns for many services.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Service mesh value proposition? Which answer is best?
options: Replacing application code with Envoy configuration only
Eliminating the need for observability tooling
Running only on bare metal without containers
Transparent mTLS, retries, metrics, and traffic policy for east-west calls
questions.explanation: Meshes push network cross-cuts into the data plane sidecars.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Blue-green deployment — how does it reduce risk? Which answer is best?
options: Switch traffic from old (blue) to new (green) environment atomically
Deploy randomly to half of canary users without metrics
Edit production binaries in place during peak traffic
Require downtime windows for every configuration change
questions.explanation: Two environments enable instant cutover and quick rollback.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Canary release distinguishing idea? Which answer is best?
options: Replace all instances simultaneously with no observation window
Shift a small % of traffic to the new version and watch error/latency signals
Only deploy on Fridays to maximize weekend soak time
Skip metrics because canaries are self-validating
questions.explanation: Canaries expose real traffic gradually before full rollout.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Feature flags help backends how? Which answer is best?
options: Remove the need for automated tests in CI
Force every change to require a database migration first
Decouple deploy from release; toggle behavior without new ship cycles
Guarantee flags never cause configuration complexity
questions.explanation: Flags let you ship dark and enable safely for cohorts.

questions.stem: Secrets management — interview expectation?
options: Keep production passwords in the mobile app binary
Email secrets to the team distribution list monthly
Hard-code API keys in Docker images for convenience
Store secrets in a vault/KMS; inject at runtime; never commit to git
questions.explanation: Central secret stores with rotation beat checked-in credentials.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: SQL injection defense baseline? Which answer is best?
options: Parameterized queries/prepared statements; never concatenate untrusted input
Escape only single quotes and ignore other characters
Disable the database user password for local trust
Rely on client-side validation alone for safety
questions.explanation: Bound parameters keep untrusted data out of the SQL parse tree.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: CORS is enforced where, fundamentally? Which answer is best?
options: By PostgreSQL rejecting foreign schemas automatically
By browsers on cross-origin frontends; APIs send Access-Control headers
Only inside mobile native apps using NSURLSession
By DNS servers blocking cross-zone HTTP entirely
questions.explanation: CORS is a browser security gate; servers declare allowed origins.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Backpressure in streaming/async systems means? Which answer is best?
options: Producers always ignore consumer speed for max throughput
Queues must be unbounded to avoid ever blocking
Slow consumers signal producers to slow down or buffer/drop controlledly
TCP flow control is unrelated to application backpressure
questions.explanation: Backpressure prevents overload by propagating slowness upstream.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Observability triad commonly cited? Which answer is best?
options: Only CPU temperature sensors on bare-metal hosts
Code coverage percentages from unit test runs
Marketing analytics dashboards for feature adoption
Metrics, logs, and traces working together to explain system behavior
questions.explanation: Golden signals plus traces/logs form modern observability.

questions.stem: Exactly-once processing in practice — honest interview take?
options: Hard end-to-end; usually at-least-once plus idempotent writes/dedupe keys
Every message broker guarantees exactly-once without app help
Turning off acks yields exactly-once automatically
Exactly-once is impossible to approximate in any system
questions.explanation: True exactly-once is elusive; idempotency is the pragmatic path.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Soft delete vs hard delete — trade-off? Which answer is best?
options: Hard delete always keeps full audit history automatically
Soft delete preserves history/recovery; hard delete reclaims space and simplifies unique keys
Soft delete never needs indexes on deleted flags
They are identical for GDPR erasure requests
questions.explanation: Choose based on audit, recovery, and compliance erasure needs.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Pagination: keyset vs offset — scalability point? Which answer is best?
options: OFFSET is always O(1) regardless of page depth
Keyset cannot paginate sorted results at all
Keyset/seek uses a stable cursor and scales better than deep OFFSET
Both strategies require loading the full table into memory
questions.explanation: Deep OFFSET scans/skips rows; keyset continues from the last key.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Multi-tenant data isolation approaches? Which answer is best?
options: Rely on clients to filter other tenants' rows in the UI only
Use one global admin password shared by all tenants
Disable row-level checks when using connection pools
Separate DB/schema, or shared tables with tenant_id and careful authz
questions.explanation: Isolation can be physical or logical — leaks are a critical risk.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Why put timeouts on every outbound call? Which answer is best?
options: Prevent thread/connection exhaustion when dependencies hang
Timeouts always make systems slower with no upside
Infinite waits maximize end-user success rates
Only inbound requests need timeouts, never outbound
questions.explanation: Hung dependencies without timeouts cascade into total outages.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Prepared statements also help performance how? Which answer is best?
options: They disable indexes so sequential scans run faster
Servers can cache query plans and skip repeated parse/optimize work
They force full table locks on every execution
They only work for DDL and never for DML
questions.explanation: Plan caching is a side benefit alongside injection safety.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Event-driven choreography vs orchestration? Which answer is best?
options: Orchestration forbids compensations; choreography requires 2PC
They are banned together in microservice style guides
Choreography: services react to events; orchestration: a coordinator drives steps
Choreography always uses a single shared database transaction
questions.explanation: Both coordinate workflows; coupling and visibility differ.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Connection drain during deploys interacts with what LB feature? Which answer is best?
options: Sticky sessions based on CPU temperature only
DNS round-robin with five-day TTLs exclusively
Disabling health checks for faster rollouts
Removing instance from pool / connection draining before SIGTERM handling
questions.explanation: LB drain + graceful shutdown avoids cutting live requests.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Bloom filters in backend caching/DBs — typical use? Which answer is best?
options: Probabilistic set membership to skip expensive lookups on definite misses
Exact counting of distinct users with zero error
Encrypting passwords before bcrypt hashing
Replacing B-tree indexes for all primary keys
questions.explanation: Bloom filters say "definitely not present" cheaply with false positives.

questions.stem: In an interview, you are asked: Why separate compute from stateful stores in cloud backends? Which answer is best?
options: Stateful disks cannot be networked in any cloud
Scale and fail app tiers independently from durable data services
Stateless stores are required by the CAP theorem
Compute and storage must share one failure domain always
questions.explanation: Decoupling lets you scale web tiers without resizing databases lockstep.
